nativeRange.habitat: Eutrophic, shallow, often temporary freshwater bodies such as ponds, ditches, rice paddies, and pools, where high concentrations of bacteria and phytoplankton support filter-feeding cladocerans.

decorPreferences: no copper or metal surfaces in contact with culture water

diet.notes: Filter feeder that has been documented consuming bacteria, single-celled algae (green water), yeast, and fine organic particles suspended in the water. Common hobbyist culture foods include active dry yeast suspension, spirulina powder, green water (algae-rich water), and boiled potato or carrot slices that fuel a bacterial bloom. Generally advised to feed sparingly and frequently rather than in large doses to avoid fouling the culture water.

goodWith: monoculture only (kept as a feed-culture, not a display animal)
harvested and fed to fry of bettas, killifish, tetras, rainbowfish, livebearers, gouramis, and most egglayers
harvested for small adult nano fish (chili rasboras, Boraras, scarlet badis)
avoidWith: any predator in the culture container
copper- or zinc-containing decor, plumbing, or medications in contact with culture water
fish that are too large to benefit from sub-millimeter prey (will simply ignore them)

summary: A tiny, warm-water freshwater cladoceran (water flea) widely cultured as a live food for fish fry and nano fish. Generally considered smaller and more prolific than the common Daphnia, and well-suited to the smaller mouth gapes of newly hatched livebearer, killifish, and tetra fry.
careNotes: Not a display species — this entry documents Moina as a feed-culture organism. Adult females typically reach roughly 0.5-1.5 mm (with reports up to ~1.8 mm under optimal conditions); newborn neonates are around 0.2-0.4 mm and are small enough for most newly-hatched livebearer, killifish, tetra, and rainbowfish fry that cannot fit larger Daphnia. Generally advised to culture at 22-30°C, with peak reproduction around 25-27°C; tolerates 4-35°C but reproduction slows dramatically below ~18°C and water quality degrades quickly above ~30°C. Reported tolerated pH range is 6.5-9.0 with optimum around 7.5-8.0. Has been reported to be extremely sensitive to copper, zinc, chlorine, chloramine, pesticides, and most aquarium medications — use dechlorinated tap water, RO/spring water, or aged aquarium water for the culture, and never use copper-plumbed water or copper-containing decor. Generally cultured in small bare-bottom containers from a 4-8 L jar or bucket up to ~20 L tank; redundant backup cultures (3-4 jars) are widely recommended because a single crash can wipe out the whole supply. Food options that have been reported as reliable include active dry yeast slurry, spirulina powder, green water (algae bloom), and bacterial blooms triggered by a small slice of boiled potato or carrot. Harvest by pouring through a fine brine-shrimp net (~150 micron) and rinsing the catch into the rearing tank. Cross-source notes: Aquariumbreeder reports lifespan of 2-3 weeks (up to 70 days in lab), tropical-fish-keeping reports adult body size of 0.02-0.04 in (~0.5-1.0 mm), and ResearchGate / Springer literature reports female maximum of 1.65-1.8 mm at 25 C with abundant food — values above are an averaged range. Designated primary (The Shrimp Farm) and secondary (Practical Fishkeeping) both returned HTTP 403 Forbidden during this research run; substituted aquariumbreeder.com (Shrimp and Snail Breeder), tropical-fish-keeping.com, and copepodsforsale.com / DRAK-Aquaristik as confirmed cross-sources, plus a Springer Hydrobiologia / ResearchGate measurement reference for adult body size triangulation. Tank-size minimum of 4 L reflects the smallest realistic hobbyist culture jar; the schema's tank.minVolumeLiters field is being used to encode the recommended culture container volume rather than a display-tank size.
breedingNotes: Reproduces extremely rapidly in culture. Under good conditions, populations are functionally all-female and reproduce by parthenogenesis (asexual), reaching sexual maturity in 4-7 days at 24-26°C. Each female has been reported to produce 4-22 neonates per brood, with broods every 1-2 days, supporting population doubling times of roughly 2-5 days. Under deteriorating conditions (food shortage, overcrowding, temperature stress, drying), the culture has been reported to switch to sexual reproduction and produce thick-shelled resting eggs called ephippia that can survive desiccation for years and are how the species is often shipped between hobbyists. To maintain a sustained supply, generally advised to keep multiple staggered cultures and refresh starter stocks every 2-4 weeks.

primary.name: The Shrimp Farm
primary.notes: Designated primary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden during this research run; substituted with aquariumbreeder.com (Shrimp and Snail Breeder) as functional primary. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden during this research run; substituted with tropical-fish-keeping.com, copepodsforsale.com, and DRAK-Aquaristik for husbandry, and ResearchGate / Springer Hydrobiologia for body-size measurements. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Aquariumbreeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder) - Moina Profile and Culture Guide
additional.notes: Functional primary substitute. Source for taxonomy (family Moinidae), temperature optimum 21-29 C, pH 7-8.5, gH 3-10, culture container 4-8 L, lifespan 2-3 weeks (up to 70 days lab), brood size 4-22 every 1.5-2 days, extreme copper/zinc sensitivity.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping - Moina (Moina macrocopa)
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed temperature 24-31 C ideal, pH 7.0-8.0, container 5-10 gal (~20-40 L), copper and chemical sensitivity, comparison to Daphnia pulex (smaller; higher protein/lower fat), open brood pouch.

additional.name: Copepodsforsale - Moina vs Daphnia
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed maturation at 4-5 days at 26 C, container 5-10 gal, NH3 < 0.2 mg/L, O2 > 3.5 mg/L, and that Moina is smaller than Daphnia magna (suitable for newborn fry).

additional.name: ResearchGate / Springer Hydrobiologia - Moina macrocopa body size data
additional.notes: Triangulation source for adult female body length: females reach ~1.65 mm typical, up to ~1.8 mm under high food at 25 C. Resolves a 2x size disagreement between aquariumbreeder (0.2-0.5 mm) and tropical-fish-keeping (0.5-1.0 mm).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Cladocerans molt very frequently as part of normal growth and reproduction — generally reported at roughly every 1-2 days during active reproduction at 25 C, with each molt typically accompanying release of a new brood from the brood pouch. Shed exoskeletons and accompanying neonates contribute fine particulates to the culture water; widely considered normal and not a sign of trouble.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Parthenogenetic under good conditions; produces ephippia (resting eggs) under stress. See top-level breedingNotes for full culture protocol.
